text: This is a video of a patient with a perforated peptic ulcer disease. This patient presented to the emergency department with abdominal pain and she had a chest x-ray that showed the free air under the diaphragm. I typically approach these patients laparoscopically and do not perform a CT of the abdomen pelvis. After resuscitation, as long as they are not hypertensive, they'll have a diagnostic laparoscopy. And I always can send these patients and inform them that they might have a persistent leak.

Following the procedure, I typically place all these patients in the French position with a little bit of a reverse Trendelenburg with the surgeon standing between the patient's legs. and the camera at the umbilical port. I moved away from using Nathan's in the retractor or any laparoscopic liver retractors and just put an additional five millimeter trocar to retract the gallbladder as I do have residents with me. So the person that's holding the camera will be retracting the gallbladder. I have been randomizing these patients to suction only versus suction and followed by irrigation and suction. I like to perform that first prior to fixing the perforation. I typically use a two-oh silk suture to repair the perforation and close it primarily regardless of how small or how big the hole is. And this is performing the interrupted fashion. And I just like using a silk because it's easier to suture with.

Of note, I'd like to say that those patients that were just suctioned without irrigation had a significant shorter hospital stay due to lack of ileus. But my sample is too small to make any scientific conclusion. Those that were suctioned followed by copious irrigation and suctioning had longer ileus.

After completing the primary repair of the perforated ulcer, the surgeon performs a leak test by connecting the NG tube to two liters of oxygen. Once confirmed there is no leak, the surgeon begins placing a patch over the repair using three sutures: silk, vicryl, and silk, ensuring the omentum adequately covers the closure.

The surgeon places a sealant over the repaired ulcer site, followed by positioning the omentum to cover the repair. If omentum is unavailable, the falciform ligament is used as an alternative for coverage.

The surgeon ties down the omentum over the repaired ulcer site, ensuring the transverse colon remains elevated by adjusting the patient's position slightly off Trendelenburg. The omentum is sutured without cutting, and the sutures are tied in the usual manner. No drains are placed, and an NG tube is used post-operatively, typically removed on the first day after surgery.

Post-op day number one, these patients are started immediately on a full liquid diet and on treatment for H. pylori eradication. And about six weeks after the Six weeks after the surgery, they'll get an EGD with a biopsy and also confirmation for H. pylori eradication. Though most of the time, I do not know if H. pylori infection in the first place. Thank you.
